content:
10 min read
1 hour ago

--

There is a particular kind of person who, when handed a wall, does not ask permission to walk through the door — they study the wall, find the weak brick, and build a passage. I have spent my life being that person, mostly because I had no choice. This blog is about a programming language I built called Shilp, but it is really about a conviction I have carried for decades: that programming was never about syntax, and that the day we stop pretending it is, a great many more people will discover they can think like programmers.
Let me start with the thing most people get wrong.

Programming Is a Way of Thinking, Not a List of Words
Ask most people why they never learned to code and they will tell you the same thing: the syntax scared them off. The semicolons, the curly braces, the cryptic error messages, the sense that you had to memorise an alien vocabulary before you could express a single idea. They walked up to that wall, saw how tall it was, and turned back.
But here is the truth that took me years to articulate clearly. Programming is not the syntax. Programming is the analytical mind — the ability to break a messy problem into clean steps, to spot a pattern, to ask “is there a better way to do this?” and then to chase that better way relentlessly. The syntax of Python or C or Java is just the particular chisel you happen to be holding. The sculpture in your head is the real thing.
I know this not from theory but from the way my own life has been structured around it.

The Discipline of Finding a Better Way
I have cerebral palsy. Among the many things this means in practice is that I cannot hold or use a computer mouse. For most people the mouse is invisible infrastructure — they reach for it without a thought. For me it was a wall.
So I did what I have always done. I looked for a better way. Fairly quickly I figured out how to move the cursor around the screen using only the keyboard — mouse keys, keyboard navigation, whatever the operating system offered. What could have been a permanent barrier became a solved problem, and that simply became the way thought and the way I work.
This habit — if I cannot do something the ordinary way, I will find and often build the better way — is the single most important thing I bring to a computer. It is also, not coincidentally, the exact mental posture that programming rewards.
Years later, I ran into a very ordinary task: I had to enter a large amount of data into an Excel sheet. For most people this is tedious but trivial. For me, given my motor limitations, it was genuinely challenging and slow. I could have gritted my teeth and pushed through. Instead I stopped, researched a little, and wrote a small Python script that did the whole job for me.
That moment is worth sitting with. I did not learn Python because I wanted to become a programmer. I learned just enough Python because I had a real problem and programming was the sharpest tool within reach. The analytical instinct came first; the syntax was something I picked up along the way, only as much as I needed. That is the correct order, and it is the order our education systems almost always gets backwards.

The Friends Who Could Have Been Programmers
Here is what convinced me this is not just my personal story.
I have a handful of friends who are, by any honest measure, as intelligent as I am — some sharper. They can reason, they can plan, they can see three moves ahead in an argument or a business decision. By the “programming is analytical thinking” definition, they already are programmers. They simply do not know it.
And yet not one of them managed to learn to code. Every one of them bounced off the same wall: the syntax. It was too confusing, too foreign, too daunting. English keywords, unfamiliar punctuation, error messages that read like accusations in a language they never fully owned. Their intelligence was never the bottleneck. The chisel we handed them simply did not fit their hand.
This is the quiet tragedy that Shilp is meant to address. If the only thing standing between an analytically gifted person and the power of programming is that the vocabulary is tied to one specific foreign language — then the vocabulary is the problem, and the vocabulary is exactly the part we can change.

Abstract the Syntax, Widen the Door
Follow the logic to its conclusion. If programming concepts are universal — loops, conditions, functions, variables — but the expression of those concepts is arbitrarily locked to English keywords, then abstracting the syntax away from any one particular language is not a gimmick. It is a door widening.
Make the concepts expressible in a language people already think in, and you do not lower the bar — you remove an artificial one. The person still has to learn to decompose a problem, still has to reason through the logic. That difficulty is real and worth keeping. What you remove is the unrelated difficulty of first mastering a foreign vocabulary just to get started.
More people cross that threshold. More people carry the analytical skill back into their own lives and work. That is the entire thesis, and it is why Shilp exists.

Programmers Are Sculptors
I called the language Shilp — शिल्प — and the name is not decoration. Shilp means craft, sculpture, the art of shaping. It also stands for Sahaj Hindi Lipi Prakalp — roughly, “the simple Hindi script project.”
I think of programmers as sculptors. A sculptor takes a raw block of stone or wood — formless, full of potential, useless as it stands — and chisels it, strike by strike, into something that serves a purpose. A programmer does exactly this with source code. The raw material is the tangle of logic in your head; the chisel is syntax; and blow by blow, line by line, you shape it into working software that does something real in the world.
If that is what programming is, then the language of the chisel should never be the reason someone cannot sculpt. A sculptor in Varanasi and a sculptor in Florence work the same stone with the same fundamental skill. Why should the programmer be forced to think in someone else’s tongue before they are allowed to carve?
Shilp is Python underneath — semantically identical to it, keyword for keyword. But its keywords, its builtins, its variables, and even its error messages are in Hindi, in Devanagari. if becomes अगर. def and return become कार्य and वापस. for ... in becomes हर ... में. print becomes छापो. When something breaks, the traceback speaks Hindi too, pointing at your own Hindi source lines. A ZeroDivisionError comes back as शून्य से भाग त्रुटि — "division by zero is not possible." The concepts are Python's, untouched and complete; the chisel simply fits an Indian hand.
And because the whole thing is a thin, reversible translation layer over CPython, every Python feature still works — classes, generators, async/await, match/case, f-strings, the entire pip ecosystem. You can even ask it to show you the generated Python (shilp anuvad), or convert Python back into Shilp. The word tables are just data, which means Marathi, Tamil, Bengali and other language packs need no changes to the engine at all. The door I widened for Hindi opens the same way for every Indian language.

The “Chalta Hai” Problem We Must Outgrow
I want to say something uncomfortable about us as a people, because I think it matters.
We Indians have a “chalta hai” attitude — it’ll do, it’s fine, don’t rock the boat. If something is handed to us, we use it happily and rarely ask whether it could be better. Innovation has, for far too long, taken a backseat. Even when there is a genuine need. Even when there are real, daily, grinding pain points staring us in the face. We adapt ourselves to broken tools instead of fixing the tools.
I do not say this to scold. I say it because I have lived the opposite instinct out of necessity — cerebral palsy left me no room for “chalta hai.” When you physically cannot use the standard tool, you are forced to ask the innovator’s question every single day: is there a better way, and can I build it? What was a constraint for me turned out to be a gift, because it made that question a reflex.
My argument is simply that everyone would benefit from adopting that reflex by choice, the way I had to adopt it by circumstance. A regional programming language is a small example of refusing “chalta hai.” Millions of Indians are told, implicitly, that to command a computer they must first surrender to English. Most just accept it. Shilp is me refusing to accept it.

AI Changed the Speed of Everything
Now here is the part that turns conviction into possibility.
For most of computing history, building a programming language was the work of dedicated teams, over years, with real budgets and institutional backing. That is precisely why the earlier attempts at regional languages look the way they do — which I will come to a bit later. The barrier to doing something about the problem was enormous.
AI has added an entirely new dimension: the breakneck speed of innovation. A single person, working alone, can now accomplish what once required a whole team and a large budget. The leverage is staggering. Shilp is a concrete example of exactly this. One person, with cerebral palsy, unable to use a mouse, designed and built a complete, working, installable Hindi programming language — tokenizer, keyword remapping, Hindi builtins, Hindi tracebacks, a REPL, a translator both ways, VS Code highlighting, differential tests against CPython, the whole lot. Not a toy. A working language you can pip install today.
That was not possible for an individual a few years ago. It is possible now. The tool that AI hands us is not the answer to any one problem — it is a force multiplier on the innovator’s instinct. And that changes who is allowed to build and who could be called Innovators.

Why Shilp Is in a Rare Position
When I first had this idea, I did the obvious thing: I googled “regional programming language” to see what already existed. The results were revealing.
Almost everything I found was a university-led project — efforts running for years, sometimes decades, backed by academic departments and research grants. And for all that time and institutional weight, none of them are complete regional-language implementations you can actually pick up and use for real work. They are papers, prototypes, partial demonstrations, worthy research that never quite crossed into a finished, installable tool in ordinary people’s hands.
That is what puts Shilp in a unique position. It is not a research prototype gesturing at what might be possible. It is a complete implementation — semantically identical to Python, feature-complete because it rides on CPython, reversible, extensible to other Indian languages, and shipped. Built not by a funded lab over a decade, but by one determined person in a fraction of that time, precisely because AI collapsed the cost and the effort of doing it.

Where Shilp Belongs
A language is only worth building if it can be used. Here is where I see Shilp making a real difference.
Education, most obviously. A student learning to program for the first time should be able to focus their entire mind on the concepts — the loop, the condition, the function — without simultaneously fighting a foreign vocabulary. Teach a child to sculpt in the language they dream in, and far fewer of them will bounce off the wall the way my friends did.
Rural setups, where English-speaking talent is scarce. There is enormous analytical ability in India’s small towns and villages that never converts into technical capability, purely because the entry vocabulary is English. Remove that specific barrier and you unlock a vast reservoir of people who could automate, build, and solve local problems in their own communities.
Government departments and PSUs, where regional-language use is often mandated. Official communication, forms, signage and documentation in many contexts are r
